name: Tamil

ancestors.notes: Direct descendant of Proto-Dravidian via Proto-South-Dravidian
Sanskrit adstrate: Grantha letters, Sanskrit loanwords in formal register

notes: Standard Tamil (Tamil Nadu, India + Sri Lanka). Dravidian language, not related to Indo-Aryan. Key features: no aspirated stop phonemes (aspiration is allophonic); voiced/voiceless distinction positional (not phonemic for native words); unique retroflex approximant ⟨ழ⟩ = [ɻ]; retroflex lateral ⟨ள⟩ = [ɭ]; alveolar ⟨ன⟩ vs dental ⟨ந⟩. Strong diglossia: literary (centamiḻ) vs spoken (kōccai tamiḻ). Oldest attested Dravidian language (inscriptions from 3rd c. BCE).

sources.author: Schiffman, H.F.
sources.title: A Reference Grammar of Spoken Tamil

sources.author: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I.
sources.title: The Sounds of the World's Languages

sources.author: Keane, E.
sources.title: Tamil
sources.publisher: Illustrations of the IPA, Journal of the International Phonetic Association 34(1)
sources.notes: The stop series has no voicing contrast: plosives are voiceless word-initially and when geminated, voiced intervocalically and after a nasal. Source for TA_GEM*, TA_NOVOICE_* and TA_VOICE_*.

allophone_rules.notes: Tamil gemination, first half. A doubled consonant letter (C + virama + C) is a phonetically LONG consonant, transcribed with the length mark (Keane 2004: 111-116, 'Tamil', Journal of the IPA 34(1); Krishnamurti 2003: ch. 4). The cluster's first member — the vowel-less consonant — carries the length: புத்தகம் [put̪ːaɡam], அக்கா [akːaː]. The second half is stripped to its bare vowel by TA_GEM2_*, so the pair rewrites C+C+V to Cː+V. A geminate is ALWAYS voiceless, which is why the geminate rules are ordered before the voicing rules: first match wins.

Tamil gemination, second half (see TA_GEM1_*): the second member of a geminate contributes only its vowel, the consonant itself having been absorbed into the length mark on the first member (Keane 2004: 111-116). Firing before the voicing rules is what keeps a geminate voiceless — பக்கம் [pakːam], never *[paɡam] (Keane 2004: 113: voicing is blocked in geminates).

Voicing guard. Tamil has NO phonemic voicing contrast in stops: voicing is positional (Keane 2004: 111-116; Krishnamurti 2003: 4.2; Wikipedia, 'Tamil phonology'). A stop is VOICELESS word-initially and when it follows another vowel-less obstruent or liquid (i.e. inside a cluster that is not nasal+stop): பார்ப்பு, கற்க. This identity rewrite fires first in exactly that context and — first match wins — blocks TA_VOICE_*. Nasals are deliberately absent from the list: a stop AFTER a nasal is voiced (TA_VOICE_*), தங்கம் [t̪aŋɡam].

Intervocalic and post-nasal voicing of the stop series — the central allophonic process of Tamil (Keane 2004: 111-116, Journal of the IPA 34(1); Krishnamurti 2003: 4.2; Schiffman 1999: ch. 1). A single plosive between vowels, or after a nasal, is voiced: புத்தகம் [put̪ːaɡam], எதிரி [ed̪iɾi], நன்கொடை [nanɡoɖai]. Every other medial context is ruled out before this rule is reached (TA_GEM*_ for geminates, TA_NOVOICE_* for obstruent/liquid clusters), and word_initial=false excludes the initial position, so what is left IS the intervocalic / post-nasal environment: the previous lattice slot is either a vowel, a consonant that carries its own vowel, or a vowel-less nasal. The palatal ⟨ச⟩ lenites to [s] between vowels but voices to [dʑ] after a nasal (Keane 2004: 113; Schiffman 1999), so it takes two rules rather than one.
Intervocalic / post-nasal voicing; see TA_VOICE_N_ka (Keane 2004: 111-116; Krishnamurti 2003: 4.2).
